You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — React Basics: Class? Hooks? — (3)
Function Component? Class Component? React Hooks? What even are these things? So in this post we're going to get to know the different ways of writing React and what makes them different.

These days React basically has two ways of writing components: Function Component and Class Component. You might not have heard the term "Function Component" before, but you've definitely heard of React Hooks — and React Hooks is exactly what we mean by Function Component. Class Component is just the older way React used to be written.
Let me borrow the Class Component example from an earlier chapter:

A Class Component is really easy to spot — it's built using ES6
syntax. And basically, the concept of a Component, according to the official docs, is just a function that holds some React Elements inside it.

If you're a Vue developer reading this, it's probably starting to feel really familiar — because Vue also gives you two ways of writing things:

You'll notice that the Composition API and React Hooks are pretty similar in places — so much so that you could basically call the Composition API "Vue Hooks." lol
And the Vue author has actually said the Composition API was mainly inspired by React Hooks, so you can really see the back-and-forth between React and Vue here.
Next let's talk about the rules and limitations you have to follow when using React Hooks.
Basically there are only two key limitations:
You can only call them at the top level of the function
You can only use them in a Function Component
Only at the top level of the function
only at the top level of the function

// ❌ Error! Breaks the Rules of Hooks
See the Pen (11) React Hooks by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
The correct way is to put it at the very top level of the function:
// ✅ Good! Follows the Rules of Hooks
See the Pen (14) React Hooks Error by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
React Hooks were designed around the Function Component, so you can only use them inside a Function Component — you can't use them in a Class Component.
See the Pen (15) React Hooks Good by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
You don't really need to memorize this, though, because if you actually try to do this, you'll see this message in the Console:
language-cmd shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Uncaught Error: Invalid hook call. Hooks can only be called inside of the body of a function component. This could happen for one of the following reasons:
1. You might have mismatching versions of React and the renderer (such as React DOM)
2. You might be breaking the Rules of Hooks
3. You might have more than one copy of React in the same app
See https://reactjs.org/link/invalid-hook-call for tips about how to debug and fix this problem.
Uncaught Error: Invalid hook call. Hooks can only be called inside of the body of a function component. This could happen for one of the following reasons:
1. You might have mismatching versions of React and the renderer (such as React DOM)
2. You might be breaking the Rules of Hooks
3. You might have more than one copy of React in the same app
See https://reactjs.org/link/invalid-hook-call for tips about how to debug and fix this problem.
On top of that, React Hooks — just like Vue 3's Composition API — is backwards compatible, so you can have both Class Component and Function Component styles living in the same project.

At this point you might be wondering: "So should I develop with Class Component, or Function Component, or both?" The official docs actually answer this, so let me just pull the explanation straight from the

We encourage you to start trying Hooks in new components you write. Make sure everyone on your team is on board with using them and familiar with this documentation. We don't recommend rewriting your existing classes to Hooks unless you planned to rewrite them anyway (e.g. to fix bugs).
You can't use Hooks inside a class component, but you can definitely mix classes and function components with Hooks in a single tree. Whether a component is a class or a function that uses Hooks is an implementation detail of that component. In the longer term, we expect Hooks to be the primary way people write React components.
So you can tell React Hooks is bound to become the mainstream way of writing React — after all, that's the direction the team itself is hoping you'll go.
